title: Cat Ate a Dried Raisin: Urgency Guide and Veterinary Triage Steps
meta_desc: If your cat ate a raisin, treat it as a high-priority emergency. Learn why raisins are toxic to cats and what triage steps to take immediately.
content_html:
What is Raisin Toxicity in Cats?

Raisins are dried grapes, and both are known to be highly toxic to felines. While the exact toxin responsible for the reaction is still being researched, ingestion often leads to acute kidney injury or complete renal failure. Because raisins are a concentrated form of the fruit, even a very small amount can be dangerous.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Any ingestion of a raisin by a cat should be treated as a medical emergency. Unlike many other toxicities, there is no known 'safe' dose for raisins; even a single dried raisin can cause fatal kidney failure in some cats. You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately rather than waiting for symptoms like vomiting or lethargy to appear.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a photo of the raisin or the product packaging can be incredibly helpful for the veterinary team. A photo helps confirm the exact item ingested and can assist the vet in identifying any other potential toxins listed in the ingredients, such as chocolate coating or xylitol. This visual information allows the triage staff to prepare the appropriate treatment room and supplies before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of raisin poisoning in cats?
faqs.answer: Initial symptoms often include vomiting, diarrhea, and decreased appetite. As kidney damage progresses, you may notice increased thirst, lethargy, or a decrease in urine production.

faqs.question: How soon after ingestion will a cat get sick?
faqs.answer: Symptoms typically begin within 6 to 12 hours of ingestion, but internal kidney damage can start much sooner. This is why immediate veterinary intervention is required.

faqs.question: Can I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never attempt to induce vomiting in a cat at home unless specifically instructed to do so by a licensed veterinarian, as it can lead to severe esophageal damage or aspiration.
